text: Robotic Diverting Colostomy Patient ML is a thirty eight year old female with grade four sacral decubitus ulcer. Past medical history is significant for encephalopathy and morbid obesity.
Past surgical history is significant for an open cholecystectomy and an open appendectomy.

The surgical team employs a four trocar technique, inserting a 12mm trocar for the camera at the umbilicus, an 8mm trocar in the right upper quadrant, another 12mm trocar in the right lower quadrant for the stapler, and a conduit for an 8mm robotic cannula.
The surgeon inserts a twelve millimeter trocar at the preoperatively marked ostomy site, preparing for the next steps in the colectomy procedure.
An extra five millimeter trocar is inserted to clear extensive omental adhesions from the abdominal wall, revealing the obscured right side of the abdomen.
The robotic surgical system is docked over the patient's left lateral abdomen, positioning the robotic arms for the colectomy procedure.

The surgeon initiates the procedure by mobilizing the sigmoid and descending colon from a lateral to medial approach, using surgical instruments to carefully separate the colon from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues the cephalad dissection towards the splenic flexure, advancing the separation of tissue layers in this region.
The surgeon ligates the inferior mesenteric artery after achieving adequate mobilization of the colon, using robotic assistance and trocars placed in the left lateral abdomen and ostomy site.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to ligate vessels down to the level of the rectum, ensuring hemostasis in the area.
The surgeon confirms adequate mobilization of the colon, visually assessing its position and tension, before proceeding with the transection of the rectum.
The surgeon uses a grasper to secure the proximal colon through a 12mm trocar site.
The surgeon uses a Proline suture to mark the rectum, preparing for future colostomy reversal.
The surgeon exteriorizes the proximal colon through the twelve millimeter trocar site, followed by maturation of the ostomy.
